From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Altos Hornos de Mexico](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_Mexico&redirect=no "Altos Hornos de Mexico"))

Steel plant in Mexico

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico "Special:EditPage/Altos Hornos de México") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Altos Hornos de México"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Altos+Hornos+de+M%C3%A9xico%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Altos+Hornos+de+M%C3%A9xico%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Altos+Hornos+de+M%C3%A9xico%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Altos+Hornos+de+M%C3%A9xico%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Altos+Hornos+de+M%C3%A9xico%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Altos+Hornos+de+M%C3%A9xico%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(April 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Altos Hornos de México S.A.B. de C.V.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Company type | [Private](/wiki/Private_company "Private company") |
| Industry | [Metals](/wiki/Metals "Metals") |
| Founded | 1942; 84 years ago (1942) |
| Headquarters | [Monclova](/wiki/Monclova "Monclova"), [Coahuila](/wiki/Coahuila "Coahuila") |
| Key people | Argentem Creek Partners |
| Products | [Steels](/wiki/Steel "Steel") [mining](/wiki/Mining "Mining") [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") |
| [Total equity](/wiki/Equity_(finance) "Equity (finance)") | Bankrupted |
| Number of employees | 22,250 (2016)[[1]](#cite_note-FTProfile-1) |
| [Subsidiaries](/wiki/Subsidiary "Subsidiary") | [List of subsidiaries](#Subsidiaries) |
| Website | [www.ahmsa.com](http://www.ahmsa.com) |

**Altos Hornos de México**, S.A.B. de C.V. (AHMSA) is a [steel plant](/wiki/Steel_plant "Steel plant") in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"). It has corporate offices in [Monclova](/wiki/Monclova "Monclova"), [Coahuila](/wiki/Coahuila "Coahuila"), in the center of the Mexican state of Coahuila, 155 miles from the [United States border.](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border")

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Teódulo Flores Calderon donated the land to AHMSA, later Harold Rudolph Pape founded Altos Hornos de Mexico in 1942 in Monclova, Coahuila for its proximity to the border, with the mines of iron located in the same state and Chihuahua, as well as with coal, located in the carboniferous region of Coahuila.

Between 1944 and 1948, the production increased from 40,000 to 100,000 tons of liquid steel per year. In 1960, the smelter increased its capacity to 2 million tons of liquid steel per year, with this being consolidated as the largest company industry in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). Throughout the decade of the '70s and after several expansions, the company achieved production of 3.75 million tons per year. In the decade of the '80s, AHMSA was nationalized by the federal government and went through several crises.

In 1991,  Altos Hornos was under new management by [Alonso Ancira](/wiki/Alonso_Ancira "Alonso Ancira"), but his tenure was marked by a series of fraudulent activities that eventually led to the bankruptcy of Altos Hornos de México (AHMSA). He orchestrated one of the largest scams in [Mexican history](/wiki/History_of_Mexico "History of Mexico") and was subsequently arrested by [Interpol](/wiki/Interpol "Interpol") on charges of fraud, corruption, and money laundering. Ancira's involvement in the [Pemex](/wiki/Pemex "Pemex"), Agro Nitrogenados, and [Odebrecht frauds](/wiki/Odebrecht_Case "Odebrecht Case") further solidified his culpability. The fraudulent transaction amounted to $500 million, with the plant itself being revealed as nothing more than a sham. Ancira is now obligated to repay the stolen funds to the [Mexican Government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_Mexico "Federal government of Mexico"), as his actions have resulted in AHMSA declaring [bankruptcy](/wiki/Bankruptcy "Bankruptcy").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

In April 2023, Ancira was ousted from the presidency of AHMSA, which is now in the hands of Argentem Creek Partners, an American fund that is facing the challenge of reviving a company on the brink of collapse. Ancira resigned along with members of the Board of Directors, including Jorge Alberto Ancira Elizondo, José Eduardo Ancira Elizondo, James Pignatelli, Juan Carlos Quintana, and Francisco Pérez Ortega.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

AHMSA was sold to Argentem Creek Partners in April 2023 and is now run by new management. Despite the sale to Argentem Creek Partners, the [steel company](/wiki/Steel_company "Steel company") continues to have a debt of $650 million.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Alonso Ancira's fraudulent activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Alonso Ancira's fraudulent activities")]

[Alonso Ancira Elizondo](/wiki/Alonso_Ancira "Alonso Ancira"), the former president of AHMSA from 1991-2023, is known for leading one of the largest [scams](/wiki/Scams "Scams") in the history of Mexico, defrauding the [Mexican government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_Mexico "Federal government of Mexico") of $500 million. He is responsible for his involvement in the [Pemex](/wiki/Pemex "Pemex"), Agro Nitrogenados, and [Odebrecht frauds](/wiki/Odebrecht_Case "Odebrecht Case"), as well as his mismanagement that led to the bankruptcy of Altos Hornos de México (AHMSA). Additionally, he has been accused of [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Mexico "Corruption in Mexico"), [tax fraud](/wiki/Tax-fraud "Tax-fraud"), and [money laundering](/wiki/Money_laundering "Money laundering"). [[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The [Pemex](/wiki/Pemex "Pemex") case originated in 2013 when Alonso Ancira, his brother Jorge Ancira of Altos Hornos de México (AHMSA), and [Emilio Lozoya](/wiki/Emilio_Lozoya_Austin "Emilio Lozoya Austin"), who served as [Pemex's](/wiki/Pemexgate "Pemexgate") director, engaged in fraudulent activities. They orchestrated the sale of a non-existent plant to the Mexican government, valued at just a few million dollars. However, in 2019, the [Superior Audit Office](/wiki/Superior_Auditor_of_the_Federation "Superior Auditor of the Federation") uncovered that the transaction had far exceeded its actual cost. Consequently, Ancira was arrested for fraud in [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain") in 2019 and subsequently extradited to [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") in 2021. [[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Furthermore, it came to light that during his management, the plant had been abandoned, with machines turned off for years. The 17,000 employees who worked at AHMSA had not received salary payments for four years.

Despite being in debt, Ancira still owes various areas of the federal government, such as the [Tax Administration Service](/wiki/Servicio_de_Administraci%C3%B3n_Tributaria "Servicio de Administración Tributaria"), [Pemex](/wiki/Pemex "Pemex"), and the [Federal Electricity Commission](/wiki/Comisi%C3%B3n_Federal_de_Electricidad "Comisión Federal de Electricidad"). AHMSA has debts with various areas of the [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_Mexico "Federal government of Mexico"), including the Tax Administration Service, Petróleos Mexicanos, [the Federal Electricity Commission](/wiki/Comisi%C3%B3n_Federal_de_Electricidad "Comisión Federal de Electricidad"), [the National Workers' Housing Fund Institute](/wiki/Instituto_del_Fondo_Nacional_de_la_Vivienda_para_los_Trabajadores "Instituto del Fondo Nacional de la Vivienda para los Trabajadores"), and the [Mexican Social Security Institute](/wiki/Mexican_Social_Security_Institute "Mexican Social Security Institute"), among others.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Ancira had faced previous arrests for his mismanagement and corrupt practices at Altos Hornos de México (AHMSA). In 2004, he and his family members were charged with tax fraud in [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico"), prompting them to flee the country after arrest warrants were issued by the [Attorney General's Office (PGR)](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_Mexico "Attorney General of Mexico"). To avoid capture, Ancira relocated to Israel on two occasions with his family and resided there for three years while being pursued by the Ministry of Finance. [[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Activity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Activity")]

Ahmsa's main steelmaking facilities and corporate offices are located in [Monclova](/wiki/Monclova "Monclova"), [Coahuila](/wiki/Coahuila "Coahuila"), close to transportation lines, raw materials supplies, principal points of export, and the company's major domestic markets.

### Primary resources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Primary resources")]

Two steel plants operate in an area of nearly 3,000 acres. AHMSA extracts coal and [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore"). The company has its own coal mines in Palau, located 70 miles from Monclova. Once extracted, the washed coal is shipped by railroad to AHMSA's coke plants.

The main source of iron ore is located in Hercules, Coahuila, a mine owned by AHMSA. From that point the iron ore is transported to AHMSA through a 180-mile pipe (called Ferroduct) that crosses the Coahuila desert. The Company also has iron ore mines in some other Mexican states.

### Products

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Products")]

AHMSA manufactures high value-added steel products. AHMSA is a national leader in the production and commercialization of flat steel products including hot rolled coil used for machinery parts, wide plate, cold rolled coil, tinplate and tin-free steel, railroad tanks and bridge constructions, structural shapes... It also produces non-flat steel products like heavy shapes.

AHMSA operates at an annual production rate of 3.5 million metric tons of liquid steel, with a workforce of 22,250 people, including its subsidiary companies.

### Subsidiaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Subsidiaries")]

As of April 2014, its subsidiaries included:[[14]](#cite_note-AHMSAwebsiteSubsList-14)

* Cia. Real de Monte y Pachuca (RDM)
* Hojalata Mexicana SA. de CV. (HOMESA)
* Linea Coahuila-Durango SA de CV
* Minera del Norte
* Nacional de Acero SA. de CV. (NASA)
* Unidad Cerro del Mercado
* Unidad Hercules
* Unidad MICARE
* Unidad MIMOSA

### Quality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Quality")]

AHMSA received the following quality labels :

* "Clean Industry" certifications issued by Mexico’s Environmental Protection Department
* Environmental prize granted by the Mexican Chamber of Mining
* Environmental Excellence Award granted by the Latin American Mining Organism

AHMSA also complies with the following standards :

* The hot rolling department was approved by the audit of the quality system based on the European Standard [EN 10025](/wiki/EN_10025 "EN 10025"):2004
* AHMSA was recertified for three more years by the [ISO 14001](/wiki/ISO_14001 "ISO 14001") standard[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]
* Integrated systems were established for quality, health and environment in Cold Rolling 1, Structural Shapes and Maintenance and Services
* MICARE, MIMOSA, CEMESA, Hércules and La Perla were approved by the [ISO 9001:2000](/wiki/ISO_9001:2000 "ISO 9001:2000") standard, without observations

Since 1992, AHMSA leads an active social policy where its plants are active, maintaining permanent programs on the following fronts:

* Installation and modernization of state-of-the-art steelworks and mining equipment that incorporates antipollution systems.
* Operating and administrative practices in line with international environmental standards and certified by accredited external auditors.
* Agreements with the Federal Environmental Protection Agency (PROFEPA) and with the National Water Commission (CNA), committing to the fulfillment of specific activities, established in the area of pollution control.
* Voluntary adoption of best environmental practices developed at the international level by the industry and certified by the ISO14001 standard.
* Integral rehabilitation of mined areas, with [soil regeneration](/wiki/Soil_regeneration "Soil regeneration") and repopulation of regional flora and fauna, as well as improvement of surroundings.
* Integral support programs for communities where AHMSA companies operate in the Central, Carboniferous, Northern and Desert regions of the state of Coahuila, particularly through waste collection programs, operation of landfills, management and use of waste water, etc.
* Creation of 5 management units for wild flora and fauna conservation, registered with permits as Animal Management Units (UMAs).

These policies and activities have positioned AHMSA and its companies as benchmarks in the field of Mexican steelworks and mining, establishing it as an example of high social responsibility

## Ticker Symbols

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Ticker Symbols")]

AHMSA; IAM (ADR)

## NAICs Codes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Altos_Hornos_de_M%C3%A9xico&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: NAICs Codes")]

* 212111 Bituminous Coal and Lignite Surface Mining
* 212112 Bituminous Coal Underground Mining
* 21221 Iron Ore Mining
* 331111 Iron & Steel Mills
* 331221 Cold-Rolled Steel Shape Manufacturing
* 331222 Steel Wire Drawing.
